region_of_origin: Singapore (fusion dish combining Japanese karaage technique with Southeast Asian Nanyang curry)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for popcorn karaage with curry sauce. FOODON_00002670 (fried chicken food product) is the closest parent. The Nanyang curry component and popcorn-bite format are not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible parent. The popcorn-bite format, karaage marinade, and Nanyang curry sauce are not represented in FoodEx2 at this level of specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 255620007 (Fried food) is the closest available parent concept. No specific code exists for karaage or Nanyang curry chicken bites.

main_ingredients: Nanyang curry sauce (coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, curry leaves)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of popcorn karaage with Nanyang curry is approximately 200 g, comprising roughly 8–12 bite-sized fried chicken pieces accompanied by a small bowl or drizzle of Nanyang curry sauce, as typically served at Flavours by Saute, Funan, Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, fried (USDA SR Legacy 05167) combined with component estimation for Nanyang curry sauce based on USDA FoodData Central entries for coconut-milk curry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists for popcorn karaage with Nanyang curry in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried chicken thigh (FDC ID 171477 / NDB 05167) with a proportional contribution from a coconut-milk-based curry sauce component (USDA FDC ID 174276, curry sauce, ready-to-eat). The dish is approximately 70% fried chicken and 30% curry sauce by weight. All values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried chicken and mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate dishes; analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken (GI ~46) and coconut curry sauce component (GI ~50), weighted average
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for popcorn karaage with Nanyang curry. GI estimated at 48 using a weighted average of: (1) battered/fried chicken GI ~46 (University of Sydney GI Database, analogous entry for fried chicken with starch coating) contributing ~70% of dish weight, and (2) coconut-milk curry sauce GI ~50 (peer-reviewed estimate from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008', Diabetes Care 2008) contributing ~30% of dish weight. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (48 × 10.8 × 200/100) / 100 = 10.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 200 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium (~35% of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals), so patients with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium soy sauce marinade where possible, and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~10.4 g per serving) and starch-battered coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: The starch-based karaage coating and coconut curry sauce contribute a moderate glycemic load of approximately 10.4 g per 200 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and account for the carbohydrate contribution (~21.6 g per serving) within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-frying and coconut milk (~4.8 g per 100 g; ~9.6 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of deep-frying oil and coconut-milk curry sauce delivers approximately 9.6 g of saturated fat per 200 g serving (~48% of the 20 g daily limit for cardiovascular risk reduction), and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, choose smaller portions, and prefer grilled or baked chicken alternatives when available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Popcorn Karaage with Nanyang Curry is a Japanese-Southeast Asian fusion dish served in Singapore, most notably at Flavours by Saute in Funan Mall. The dish consists of bite-sized pieces of chicken (typically thigh meat) that are marinated in a Japanese karaage-style mixture of soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and sake, then coated in potato starch or cornstarch and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The 'popcorn' descriptor refers to the small, irregular bite-sized format of the fried chicken pieces, reminiscent of popcorn chicken. These crispy morsels are served alongside or drizzled with a Nanyang curry sauce — a Southeast Asian-style curry made with coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, and curry leaves, reflecting the Peranakan and Malay culinary heritage of the Nanyang (South Seas) region. The result is a textural and flavour contrast between the crunchy, umami-rich fried chicken and the aromatic, mildly spicy, creamy curry sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~235 kcal per 100 g), provides a meaningful protein contribution (~16.5 g per 100 g) from the chicken, and contains moderate saturated fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) from the frying oil and coconut milk. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the soy sauce marinade and curry seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low), though the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at ~10.4 g. Clinically, the dish warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (starch coating and curry carbohydrates), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from coconut milk and frying). It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or soy allergies without modification.
common_user_queries: How many calories in popcorn karaage?
Is popcorn karaage high in sodium?
What is the difference between karaage and regular fried chicken?
What does Nanyang mean in food?
Where can I find popcorn karaage in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for popcorn karaage with Nanyang curry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried chicken thigh with a proportional coconut-milk curry sauce component. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477, fried chicken thigh) and USDA FoodData Central curry sauce component (FDC ID 174276), weighted 70:30 chicken-to-curry. No exact match exists in any primary regional database. 2. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried chicken thigh at this level of specificity. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried chicken thigh at this level of specificity. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the combined component estimation with sufficient confidence. 6. Halal status is listed as false because the restaurant (Flavours by Saute) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment on the MUIS Singapore halal directory as of the processing date; consumers should verify directly. 7. The dish name uses the stylised capitalisation 'KaRAHage' in the source field survey listing; the canonical English name normalises this to 'Karaage'. 8. Allergen information is indicative; cross-contamination risks in a restaurant kitchen environment should be verified with the establishment. 9. Price preserved from input: SGD 10.70.
